sources.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology (Parker, Schneegurt, Tu, Lister, Forster)
sources.note: Multiple-choice Review Questions scraped verbatim; correctAnswer taken from OpenStax published Answer Key pages (pages/chapter-N). OpenStax posts a 'no LLM training' notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing per PRD, with attribution and non-commercial/share-alike compliance.

meta.notes: File 02: OpenStax Microbiology Ch. 7 (Microbial Biochemistry) and Ch. 8 (Microbial Metabolism). One 5-option 'all of the above' item (Ch.7 Q5) and microbe-identification-method items (Ch.7 Q10,Q12-14) were skipped to keep MCAT relevance and clean A-D mapping. Options 1-4 map to A-D; correctAnswer letters verified against the chapter answer keys.

questions.stem: Which of these elements is not a micronutrient?
questions.explanation: Carbon (C) is a macronutrient, needed in large amounts as the backbone of all organic molecules. Calcium, cobalt, and copper are micronutrients (trace elements) required only in small quantities.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 7 Microbial Biochemistry — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-7), Ch.7 MC Q1 = A.

hints.prompt: What distinguishes a macronutrient from a micronutrient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A macronutrient is required in large amounts; a micronutrient only in trace amounts

choices.label: B
choices.text: A macronutrient is a metal; a micronutrient is a nonmetal

choices.label: C
choices.text: A macronutrient is toxic; a micronutrient is harmless

choices.label: D
choices.text: A macronutrient is inorganic; a micronutrient is organic

hints.rationale: Macronutrients are needed in large quantities; micronutrients only in traces.

hints.prompt: Trace micronutrients are most often which kind of element?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Metal ions the cell needs in tiny amounts (often as enzyme cofactors)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Elements needed in bulk to build biomolecules

hints.rationale: Micronutrients are typically trace metal ions used as cofactors.

hints.prompt: Which two are transition-metal ions the cell uses only in tiny amounts as enzyme cofactors, so both are clearly micronutrients that can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: Cobalt and copper are trace transition-metal cofactors, unmistakably micronutrients.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the name for molecules whose structures are nonsuperimposable mirror images?
questions.explanation: Enantiomers are chiral molecules that are nonsuperimposable mirror images of each other. Structural isomers differ in connectivity, not handedness.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 7 Microbial Biochemistry — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-7), Ch.7 MC Q2 = D.

hints.prompt: Two molecules that are mirror images which cannot be superimposed on each other illustrate which general phenomenon?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Chirality (handedness), like left and right hands

hints.rationale: Nonsuperimposable mirror images reflect chirality (handedness).

hints.prompt: How do mirror-image isomers differ from ordinary structural isomers?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Mirror-image isomers share the same connectivity but have opposite handedness; structural isomers differ in how atoms are connected

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are identical in every way

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mirror-image isomers are made of different elements

choices.label: D
choices.text: Structural isomers are always larger molecules

hints.rationale: Mirror-image isomers keep connectivity but flip handedness, unlike structural isomers.

hints.prompt: Which two terms describe molecular SIZE/assembly (single units versus long chains) rather than any kind of isomerism, so they can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: Monomers (single units) and polymers (long chains) describe assembly, not mirror-image isomerism.

questions.stem: By definition, carbohydrates contain which elements?
questions.explanation: Carbohydrates are made of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen, typically in the ratio (CH2O)n.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 7 Microbial Biochemistry — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-7), Ch.7 MC Q3 = C.

hints.prompt: Carbohydrates are the class of biomolecules that includes sugars and starches. Which statement about them is true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They are a class of biomolecules that includes sugars

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are primarily made of metal ions

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are a type of protein

hints.rationale: Carbohydrates are the biomolecule class that includes sugars and starches.

hints.prompt: Does the basic chemical definition of a carbohydrate include nitrogen among its elements?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No - nitrogen is characteristic of proteins and nucleic acids, not basic carbohydrates

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes - all carbohydrates must contain nitrogen

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes - nitrogen is the main element of sugars

hints.rationale: Basic carbohydrates do not contain nitrogen; nitrogen marks proteins and nucleic acids.

hints.prompt: Which two options can be eliminated - one because it wrongly includes nitrogen, and one because it lists only carbon and hydrogen (omitting oxygen)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'carbon, hydrogen, and nitrogen' and 'carbon and hydrogen'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen' and 'carbon and oxygen'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'carbon, hydrogen, and nitrogen' and 'carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen'

hints.rationale: One option adds nitrogen (foreign to carbohydrates) and one lists only carbon and hydrogen; both can be ruled out.

questions.stem: Monosaccharides may link together to form polysaccharides by forming which type of bond?
questions.explanation: Monosaccharides are joined by glycosidic bonds formed through dehydration (condensation) reactions to build di- and polysaccharides.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 7 Microbial Biochemistry — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-7), Ch.7 MC Q4 = D.

hints.prompt: When monomers join to build a biological polymer, they are linked by what kind of bond formed through a dehydration (condensation) reaction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A strong covalent bond specific to that polymer type

hints.rationale: Monomers are joined by covalent bonds formed during dehydration reactions.

hints.prompt: Peptide bonds join amino acids and phosphodiester bonds join nucleotides. By analogy, how is the bond that joins sugar monomers best identified?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is named after the class of monomer it links (the sugar units)

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is the same peptide bond used in proteins

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is merely a hydrogen bond like those between water molecules

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is an ionic bond between charged sugars

hints.rationale: Like peptide/phosphodiester bonds, the sugar-joining bond is named for its monomer type.

hints.prompt: Which two options are NONcovalent interactions, too weak to be the backbone bond linking sugars into a polymer?
hints.rationale: Hydrogen and ionic interactions are noncovalent and too weak to form a polymer backbone.

questions.stem: Molecules bearing both polar and nonpolar groups are said to be which of the following?
questions.explanation: Amphipathic molecules (e.g., phospholipids) have both a polar/hydrophilic region and a nonpolar/hydrophobic region.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 7 Microbial Biochemistry — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-7), Ch.7 MC Q6 = B.

hints.prompt: In terms of water interaction, polar groups and nonpolar groups behave how, respectively?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Polar groups are water-attracting (hydrophilic); nonpolar groups are water-repelling (hydrophobic)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Polar groups are water-repelling; nonpolar groups are water-attracting

hints.rationale: Polar groups are hydrophilic; nonpolar groups are hydrophobic.

hints.prompt: A single molecule that contains BOTH a water-attracting region AND a water-repelling region has what kind of character?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A dual character - part water-loving and part water-fearing

choices.label: D
choices.text: No interaction with water at all

hints.rationale: Having both regions gives the molecule a dual character.

hints.prompt: Which two terms each describe only a SINGLE (pure) water behavior, so neither can describe a molecule with both polar and nonpolar parts?
hints.rationale: 'Hydrophilic' means entirely water-loving and 'hydrophobic' entirely water-fearing; neither captures having both.

questions.stem: Which of the following groups varies among different amino acids?
questions.explanation: All amino acids share an amino group, a carboxyl group, and an alpha-hydrogen; only the R group (side chain) differs among the 20 standard amino acids.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 7 Microbial Biochemistry — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-7), Ch.7 MC Q7 = C.

hints.prompt: What gives the 20 standard amino acids their different chemical properties?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Differences in one part of their structure between them

choices.label: C
choices.text: Different numbers of atoms in their carboxyl group

choices.label: D
choices.text: Being made of entirely different elements

hints.rationale: Amino acids differ at one part of their structure, giving distinct properties.

hints.prompt: Every amino acid has the same amino group and the same carboxyl group forming a common backbone. Can a part that is IDENTICAL in all amino acids be the one that VARIES?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No - a shared, identical part cannot be the variable one

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes - the carboxyl group changes in each amino acid

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes - the amino group changes in each amino acid

choices.label: D
choices.text: Only if the amino acid carries a charge

hints.rationale: The amino and carboxyl groups are identical in all amino acids, so neither can be the variable part.

hints.prompt: Which two options are part of the identical shared backbone (present unchanged in every amino acid), so neither is the variable group?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the carboxyl group and the amino group

choices.label: B
choices.text: the R group and the hydrogen atom

choices.label: C
choices.text: the carboxyl group and the R group

choices.label: D
choices.text: the amino group and the R group

hints.rationale: The carboxyl and amino groups form the shared backbone present in every amino acid.

questions.stem: The amino acids present in proteins differ in which of the following?
questions.explanation: Because their R groups differ, amino acids vary in size, shape, and chemical properties of their side groups — all of the above.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 7 Microbial Biochemistry — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-7), Ch.7 MC Q8 = D.

hints.prompt: Amino acids share the same backbone but differ at one position. Which part is responsible for the differences among them?
hints.rationale: The side chain (R group) is the variable part that distinguishes amino acids.

hints.prompt: Because only the side chain differs between amino acids, the observable differences among amino acids ultimately trace back to what?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Differences in their backbone amino groups

hints.rationale: All variation among amino acids arises from their differing side chains.

hints.prompt: Among the options, which two are purely PHYSICAL/geometric descriptors (as opposed to a chemical description of the side chain), and so each captures only one facet of the differences?

choices.label: B
choices.text: side groups and all of the above

choices.label: D
choices.text: shape and all of the above

hints.rationale: Size and shape are each only one physical aspect of the differences, so neither alone is the full answer.

questions.stem: Which of the following bonds are not involved in tertiary structure?
questions.explanation: Peptide bonds link amino acids in the primary structure backbone. Tertiary structure is stabilized by ionic bonds, hydrophobic interactions, hydrogen bonds, and disulfide bridges among side chains.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 7 Microbial Biochemistry — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-7), Ch.7 MC Q9 = A.

hints.prompt: Tertiary structure is the overall 3-D fold of a protein. It is stabilized mainly by interactions between which parts of the amino acids?
hints.rationale: Tertiary structure arises from interactions among side chains.

hints.prompt: Which type of bond forms the covalent BACKBONE that defines a protein's primary structure (its amino acid sequence)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The covalent bond linking one amino acid to the next

choices.label: B
choices.text: A weak hydrogen bond between side chains

choices.label: C
choices.text: An ionic attraction between charged side chains

choices.label: D
choices.text: A hydrophobic clustering of nonpolar side chains

hints.rationale: The covalent bond joining amino acids forms the primary-structure backbone, not a tertiary side-chain interaction.

hints.prompt: Which two options are noncovalent attractions between polar or charged side chains that DO help stabilize tertiary structure (so neither is the exception)?
hints.rationale: Ionic and hydrogen bonds are noncovalent attractions among side chains that stabilize the fold.

questions.stem: Proteomic analysis is a methodology that deals with which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the analysis of proteins functioning as enzymes within the cell

choices.label: B
choices.text: analysis of transport proteins in the cell

choices.label: C
choices.text: the analysis of integral proteins of the cell membrane

choices.label: D
choices.text: the study of all accumulated proteins of an organism

questions.explanation: Proteomics is the large-scale study of the proteome — all of the proteins expressed/accumulated by an organism.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 7 Microbial Biochemistry — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-7), Ch.7 MC Q11 = D.

hints.prompt: In biology, the suffix '-ome' (as in genome or proteome) refers to which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The COMPLETE set of something in an organism

hints.rationale: '-ome' denotes the entire/complete set (e.g., genome = all genes).

hints.prompt: A student claims proteomics studies only the cell's enzymes. Why is that too narrow?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Enzymes are just one functional subset of all the cell's proteins

choices.label: D
choices.text: Because enzymes are studied only by genomics

hints.rationale: Enzymes are one subset of proteins, so limiting proteomics to enzymes is too narrow.

hints.prompt: Which two options describe only a narrow SUBSET of proteins defined by a particular function, so both are too limited?

choices.label: A
choices.text: proteins functioning as enzymes, and transport proteins

choices.label: B
choices.text: all accumulated proteins, and integral membrane proteins

choices.label: D
choices.text: transport proteins, and all accumulated proteins

hints.rationale: Enzymatic and transport proteins are specific functional subsets, too narrow for proteomics.

questions.stem: Which of the following is an organism that obtains its energy from the transfer of electrons originating from chemical compounds and its carbon from an inorganic source?
questions.explanation: A chemoautotroph (chemolithoautotroph) derives energy from chemical compounds (electron transfer) and carbon from an inorganic source such as CO2.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 8 Microbial Metabolism — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-8), Ch.8 MC Q1 = A.

hints.prompt: In microbial nutrition terms, the 'chemo-/photo-' prefix and the '-auto-/-hetero-' part describe which two things, respectively?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The ENERGY source (chemicals vs light) and the CARBON source (inorganic vs organic)

hints.rationale: Chemo/photo = energy source; auto/hetero = carbon source.

hints.prompt: The organism obtains ENERGY from the transfer of electrons originating in chemical compounds. Which energy-source prefix does that require?
hints.rationale: Energy from chemical compounds corresponds to the 'chemo-' prefix.

hints.prompt: Since the energy comes from chemicals (not light), which two 'photo-' options can be eliminated immediately?
hints.rationale: Both 'photo-' organisms use light for energy, but this organism uses chemical energy, so both are ruled out.

questions.stem: Which of the following molecules is reduced?
questions.explanation: NADPH is the reduced (electron-carrying) form. NAD+, FAD, and O2 are oxidized forms that can accept electrons.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 8 Microbial Metabolism — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-8), Ch.8 MC Q2 = D.

hints.prompt: In redox chemistry, a molecule that is 'reduced' has done which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Gained electrons (often carried as hydrogen atoms)

hints.rationale: Reduction is the gain of electrons (frequently carried as H).

hints.prompt: Electron carriers cycle between oxidized and reduced forms. Which member of a redox pair is the REDUCED (electron-loaded) form?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The form carrying extra electrons/hydrogens, ready to donate them

choices.label: B
choices.text: The empty form that still needs to pick up electrons

choices.label: C
choices.text: Molecular oxygen, the final electron acceptor

hints.rationale: The reduced form is the electron-loaded partner ready to donate electrons.

hints.prompt: Which two options are the OXIDIZED forms of coenzymes (their reduced partners would be NADH and FADH2)?
hints.rationale: NAD+ and FAD are oxidized coenzymes; when reduced they become NADH and FADH2.

questions.stem: Enzymes work by which of the following?
questions.explanation: Enzymes are catalysts that speed reactions by lowering the activation energy; they do not change whether a reaction is exergonic or endergonic.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 8 Microbial Metabolism — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-8), Ch.8 MC Q3 = B.

hints.prompt: What is 'activation energy'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The energy barrier that must be overcome for a reaction to proceed

choices.label: B
choices.text: The total energy released by a reaction

choices.label: D
choices.text: The heat lost to the surroundings

hints.rationale: Activation energy is the barrier reactants must surmount to react.

hints.prompt: An enzyme is a catalyst: it changes the RATE of a reaction but not whether the reaction is energetically favorable. So an enzyme affects which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: How easily the reaction gets started (its energy barrier)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Whether an exergonic reaction becomes endergonic

choices.label: C
choices.text: Whether an endergonic reaction becomes exergonic

choices.label: D
choices.text: The overall free-energy change of the reaction

hints.rationale: Catalysts affect the reaction's barrier/rate, not its thermodynamic favorability.

hints.prompt: Which two options are wrong because they claim an enzyme can FLIP a reaction's thermodynamics (its exergonic/endergonic nature)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'making exergonic reactions endergonic' and 'making endergonic reactions exergonic'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'reducing the activation energy' and 'increasing the activation energy'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'reducing the activation energy' and 'making endergonic reactions exergonic'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'increasing the activation energy' and 'making exergonic reactions endergonic'

hints.rationale: Enzymes never change whether a reaction is exergonic or endergonic, so both 'flip' options are wrong.

questions.stem: To which of the following does a competitive inhibitor most structurally resemble?
questions.explanation: A competitive inhibitor resembles the substrate and competes with it for binding at the active site.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 8 Microbial Metabolism — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-8), Ch.8 MC Q4 = C.

hints.prompt: A competitive inhibitor blocks an enzyme by binding where?

choices.label: A
choices.text: In the active site, competing with the normal substrate

choices.label: B
choices.text: At a distant site, never near the substrate

hints.rationale: A competitive inhibitor competes for the active site.

hints.prompt: To compete for the active site, a competitive inhibitor must structurally mimic which molecule that normally binds there?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The molecule the enzyme normally acts on

hints.rationale: It must resemble the enzyme's normal binding partner to occupy the active site.

hints.prompt: Which two options are LOCATIONS/sites on the enzyme rather than a molecule that would occupy the active site, so they can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the active site and the allosteric site

choices.label: C
choices.text: the active site and the substrate

choices.label: D
choices.text: the allosteric site and a coenzyme

hints.rationale: The active site and allosteric site are regions of the enzyme, not molecules resembling the substrate.

questions.stem: Which of the following are organic molecules that help enzymes work correctly?
questions.explanation: Coenzymes are organic helper molecules (often vitamin-derived). Cofactors may be inorganic ions; a holoenzyme is the active enzyme plus its cofactor, and an apoenzyme is the protein lacking its cofactor.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 8 Microbial Metabolism — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-8), Ch.8 MC Q5 = B.

hints.prompt: Some enzymes need a nonprotein 'helper' to function. What is the general (umbrella) term for such a helper, which may be an inorganic ion OR an organic molecule?
hints.rationale: 'Cofactor' is the umbrella term for enzyme helper molecules/ions.

hints.prompt: Among enzyme helpers, what distinguishes the ORGANIC ones (often derived from vitamins) from the inorganic metal-ion helpers?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They are carbon-based (organic) helper molecules, unlike inorganic ion helpers

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are the protein part of the enzyme itself

choices.label: D
choices.text: They are the complete, active enzyme

hints.rationale: The organic helpers are carbon-based molecules, distinct from metal-ion helpers.

hints.prompt: Which two options name a FORM OF THE ENZYME itself rather than a helper molecule, so they can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: A holoenzyme is the complete enzyme-plus-helper and an apoenzyme is the protein missing its helper; both are enzyme forms, not helpers.

questions.stem: During which of the following is ATP not made by substrate-level phosphorylation?
questions.explanation: The transition reaction (pyruvate to acetyl-CoA) yields NADH and CO2 but no ATP. Glycolysis, the Krebs cycle, and the Entner-Doudoroff pathway each make ATP by substrate-level phosphorylation.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 8 Microbial Metabolism — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-8), Ch.8 MC Q6 = B.

hints.prompt: Substrate-level phosphorylation produces ATP by which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Directly transferring a phosphate group to ADP during a specific reaction step

choices.label: B
choices.text: Using a proton gradient across a membrane

hints.rationale: Substrate-level phosphorylation makes ATP by direct phosphate transfer to ADP.

hints.prompt: An oxidative decarboxylation reaction removes a CO2 and reduces NAD+ to NADH. By itself, does such a step generate ATP through substrate-level phosphorylation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No - it yields NADH and CO2, but not ATP

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes - it always makes two ATP

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes - it makes ATP using light

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes - it makes ATP from a proton gradient

hints.rationale: Oxidative decarboxylation produces NADH and CO2, not ATP by substrate-level phosphorylation.

hints.prompt: Which two options are glucose-splitting pathways (glucose to pyruvate) that each net ATP by substrate-level phosphorylation, so both can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the Embden-Meyerhof pathway and the Entner-Doudoroff pathway

choices.label: B
choices.text: the transition reaction and the Krebs cycle

choices.label: C
choices.text: the Embden-Meyerhof pathway and the transition reaction

choices.label: D
choices.text: the Krebs cycle and the transition reaction

hints.rationale: Both the Embden-Meyerhof and Entner-Doudoroff pathways split glucose to pyruvate and make ATP by substrate-level phosphorylation.

questions.stem: Which of the following products is made during Embden-Meyerhof glycolysis?
questions.explanation: Glycolysis (the Embden-Meyerhof pathway) splits glucose into two molecules of pyruvate, while also producing ATP and NADH.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 8 Microbial Metabolism — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-8), Ch.8 MC Q7 = B.

hints.prompt: Glycolysis is best summarized as which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The splitting of six-carbon glucose into smaller three-carbon molecules

choices.label: B
choices.text: The complete oxidation of glucose to CO2 and water

choices.label: C
choices.text: The synthesis of glucose from CO2

hints.rationale: Glycolysis splits six-carbon glucose into smaller three-carbon molecules.

hints.prompt: Glucose (6 carbons) is split into two identical 3-carbon molecules at the end of glycolysis. What is that three-carbon end product?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A three-carbon acid that feeds the transition reaction

hints.rationale: Glycolysis ends in a three-carbon product that feeds the transition reaction.

hints.prompt: Which two options are produced only LATER (in the transition reaction or Krebs cycle), not during glycolysis, so they can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: carbon dioxide (CO2) and the two-carbon acetyl group

choices.label: D
choices.text: NAD+ and the two-carbon acetyl group

hints.rationale: CO2 and the two-carbon acetyl group are generated after glycolysis (transition reaction/Krebs cycle), not within it.

questions.stem: During the catabolism of glucose, which of the following is produced only in the Krebs cycle?
questions.explanation: FADH2 is generated in the Krebs (citric acid) cycle but not during glycolysis. ATP and NADH are produced in both glycolysis and the Krebs cycle.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 8 Microbial Metabolism — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-8), Ch.8 MC Q8 = D.

hints.prompt: For a molecule to be produced ONLY in the Krebs cycle during glucose catabolism, what must be true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It must not also be produced during glycolysis

choices.label: B
choices.text: It must be produced in the largest amounts

hints.rationale: 'Only in Krebs' means it is absent from the glycolysis output.

hints.prompt: Which stage of glucose catabolism fully oxidizes the two-carbon acetyl group and includes a flavin-dependent oxidation step?
hints.rationale: The Krebs cycle fully oxidizes acetyl groups and includes a flavin-dependent (FADH2-producing) oxidation.

hints.prompt: Which two options can be eliminated - one because it is also made during glycolysis, and one because it is not a product of glucose catabolism at all?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ATP (also made in glycolysis) and NADPH (not made in glucose catabolism)

hints.rationale: ATP is also produced in glycolysis and NADPH is a biosynthetic/photosynthetic carrier, so neither is uniquely a Krebs product.

questions.stem: Which of the following is not a name for the cycle resulting in the conversion of a two-carbon acetyl to one ATP, two CO2, one FADH2, and three NADH molecules?
questions.explanation: The Krebs cycle is also called the tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle and the citric acid cycle. The Calvin cycle is the carbon-fixation phase of photosynthesis, not a name for the Krebs cycle.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 8 Microbial Metabolism — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-8), Ch.8 MC Q9 = C.

hints.prompt: A cycle that OXIDIZES a two-carbon acetyl group, releasing CO2 and producing reduced carriers (NADH, FADH2), belongs to which type of metabolism?
hints.rationale: Oxidizing acetyl and making reduced carriers is catabolic respiration.

hints.prompt: The respiration cycle in the stem has multiple synonymous names. Which of the following instead names the carbon-FIXING cycle of photosynthesis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A cycle that builds sugar from CO2 using ATP and NADPH

choices.label: B
choices.text: A cycle named for having three carboxyl-acid groups

choices.label: C
choices.text: A cycle named after Hans Krebs

choices.label: D
choices.text: A cycle named for citric acid

hints.rationale: The photosynthetic carbon-fixation cycle builds sugar from CO2, unlike the respiratory cycle's synonyms.

hints.prompt: Which two names are well-known synonyms for the SAME respiratory (acetyl-oxidizing) cycle, so neither can be the odd one out?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Krebs cycle and citric acid cycle

choices.label: B
choices.text: Calvin cycle and tricarboxylic acid cycle

choices.label: D
choices.text: citric acid cycle and Calvin cycle

hints.rationale: 'Krebs cycle' and 'citric acid cycle' both name the same respiratory cycle.

questions.stem: Which is the location of electron transport systems in prokaryotes?
questions.explanation: Prokaryotes lack mitochondria, so their electron transport systems are located in the cytoplasmic (plasma) membrane.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 8 Microbial Metabolism — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-8), Ch.8 MC Q10 = D.

hints.prompt: Which statement about prokaryotic cells is true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They lack mitochondria and other membrane-bound organelles

choices.label: C
choices.text: They have a nucleus enclosing their DNA

choices.label: D
choices.text: They have both chloroplasts and mitochondria

hints.rationale: Prokaryotes have no mitochondria or other membrane-bound organelles.

hints.prompt: Since prokaryotes have no mitochondria, which of the following can be immediately ruled out as a location inside a prokaryote?
hints.rationale: With no mitochondria, no mitochondrial membrane can exist in a prokaryote.

hints.prompt: Which two locations do NOT exist in a prokaryotic cell, so both can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the inner mitochondrial membrane and the outer mitochondrial membrane

choices.label: B
choices.text: the cytoplasm and the cytoplasmic membrane

choices.label: C
choices.text: the cytoplasmic membrane and the inner mitochondrial membrane

choices.label: D
choices.text: the cytoplasm and the outer mitochondrial membrane

hints.rationale: Prokaryotes have no mitochondria, so neither mitochondrial membrane exists in them.

questions.stem: Which is the source of the energy used to make ATP by oxidative phosphorylation?
questions.explanation: Oxidative phosphorylation uses the proton motive force (the electrochemical H+ gradient built by the electron transport chain) to drive ATP synthase.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 8 Microbial Metabolism — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-8), Ch.8 MC Q11 = C.

hints.prompt: In oxidative phosphorylation, ATP synthase makes ATP as ions flow back across a membrane. What builds up to drive this?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An electrochemical gradient of protons (H+) across the membrane

hints.rationale: A proton (H+) electrochemical gradient drives ATP synthase.

hints.prompt: Oxidative phosphorylation makes ATP by 'chemiosmosis.' Chemiosmosis refers to which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ions flowing down a gradient across a membrane to drive ATP synthase

choices.label: B
choices.text: Direct transfer of a phosphate from a substrate to ADP

hints.rationale: Chemiosmosis is ion flow down a membrane gradient powering ATP synthase.

hints.prompt: Which two options are merely reactants/participants in ATP formation (a final electron acceptor and a raw material added to ADP), not the energy SOURCE driving ATP synthase?

choices.label: B
choices.text: the proton motive force and high-energy phosphate bonds

choices.label: C
choices.text: oxygen and the proton motive force

choices.label: D
choices.text: inorganic phosphate (Pi) and the proton motive force

hints.rationale: Oxygen is the terminal electron acceptor and Pi is the phosphate added to ADP; neither is the driving energy source.

questions.stem: A cell might perform anaerobic respiration for which of the following reasons?

choices.label: B
choices.text: It lacks the transition reaction to convert pyruvate to acetyl-CoA.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It lacks Krebs cycle enzymes for processing acetyl-CoA to CO2.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It lacks a cytochrome oxidase for passing electrons to oxygen.

questions.explanation: Anaerobic respiration uses a final electron acceptor other than oxygen. A cell relies on it when it cannot pass electrons to O2 (e.g., it lacks cytochrome oxidase) or when oxygen is unavailable.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 8 Microbial Metabolism — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-8), Ch.8 MC Q12 = D.

hints.prompt: How does anaerobic respiration differ from aerobic respiration?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It uses a final electron acceptor other than oxygen

choices.label: B
choices.text: It does not use an electron transport chain at all

choices.label: D
choices.text: It occurs only in the nucleus

hints.rationale: Anaerobic respiration uses a terminal electron acceptor other than O2.

hints.prompt: A cell would switch to a non-oxygen electron acceptor specifically when it cannot do which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Deliver its electron-transport-chain electrons onto oxygen

hints.rationale: If a cell cannot pass its ETC electrons to O2, it must use an alternative acceptor.

hints.prompt: Which two reasons describe a missing UPSTREAM acetyl-processing step (rather than a reason to switch the terminal electron acceptor), so they can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: lacking the transition reaction (pyruvate to acetyl-CoA) and lacking Krebs cycle enzymes

choices.label: B
choices.text: lacking glucose and lacking a cytochrome oxidase

choices.label: C
choices.text: lacking glucose and lacking Krebs cycle enzymes

choices.label: D
choices.text: lacking the transition reaction and lacking a cytochrome oxidase

hints.rationale: Missing the transition reaction or Krebs enzymes hampers central metabolism generally; neither specifically explains using a non-oxygen terminal acceptor.

questions.stem: In prokaryotes, which of the following is true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: As electrons are transferred through an ETS, H+ is pumped out of the cell.

choices.label: B
choices.text: As electrons are transferred through an ETS, H+ is pumped into the cell.

choices.label: C
choices.text: As protons are transferred through an ETS, electrons are pumped out of the cell.

choices.label: D
choices.text: As protons are transferred through an ETS, electrons are pumped into the cell.

questions.explanation: In prokaryotes the electron transport system pumps H+ out across the plasma membrane as electrons move down the chain, establishing the proton motive force.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 8 Microbial Metabolism — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-8), Ch.8 MC Q13 = A.

hints.prompt: In an electron transport system, what is actually PUMPED across the membrane as a result of electron flow?
hints.rationale: Electron flow through the ETS drives the pumping of protons (H+) across the membrane.

hints.prompt: Why must the H+ be moved to ONE side of the membrane rather than staying evenly distributed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To build up a concentration/charge gradient that stores energy

choices.label: B
choices.text: To destroy all of the H+

choices.label: C
choices.text: To make the inside and outside electrically identical

hints.rationale: Pumping H+ to one side stores energy as an electrochemical gradient.

hints.prompt: Which two options are wrong because they claim ELECTRONS (rather than protons) get pumped across the membrane?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'as protons are transferred, electrons are pumped out' and 'as protons are transferred, electrons are pumped in'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'as electrons are transferred, H+ is pumped out' and 'as electrons are transferred, H+ is pumped in'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'as electrons are transferred, H+ is pumped out' and 'as protons are transferred, electrons are pumped in'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'as protons are transferred, electrons are pumped out' and 'as electrons are transferred, H+ is pumped in'

hints.rationale: The ETS transfers electrons and pumps protons; options saying electrons are pumped reverse the roles.

questions.stem: Which of the following is not an electron carrier within an electron transport system?
questions.explanation: ATP synthase uses the proton gradient to make ATP; it is not an electron carrier. Flavoproteins, ubiquinone, and cytochrome oxidase all transfer electrons within the ETS.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 8 Microbial Metabolism — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-8), Ch.8 MC Q14 = B.

hints.prompt: What is the role of an 'electron carrier' in an electron transport system?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It accepts electrons and passes them to the next component

choices.label: B
choices.text: It synthesizes ATP from ADP and Pi

choices.label: D
choices.text: It transports glucose into the cell

hints.rationale: Electron carriers accept and pass electrons down the chain.

hints.prompt: One listed component does NOT carry electrons; instead it uses the proton gradient to make ATP. What is that component's actual function?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Using proton flow to phosphorylate ADP into ATP

choices.label: C
choices.text: Shuttling electrons via a flavin group

choices.label: D
choices.text: Carrying electrons as a mobile quinone

hints.rationale: The odd component uses proton flow to make ATP rather than carrying electrons.

hints.prompt: Which two components are genuine electron carriers (one a mobile quinone, one the complex that passes electrons to oxygen), so neither is the exception?
hints.rationale: Ubiquinone shuttles electrons and cytochrome oxidase passes them to oxygen; both are electron carriers.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the purpose of fermentation?

choices.label: B
choices.text: to make carbon molecule intermediates for anabolism

questions.explanation: Fermentation regenerates NAD+ by oxidizing the NADH made in glycolysis, allowing glycolysis (and its small ATP yield) to continue when respiration is not possible.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 8 Microbial Metabolism — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-8), Ch.8 MC Q15 = D.

hints.prompt: Glycolysis requires a supply of NAD+ to keep running. What happens to NAD+ during glycolysis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is reduced to NADH and must be regenerated for glycolysis to continue

hints.rationale: Glycolysis converts NAD+ to NADH, so NAD+ must be regenerated to sustain glycolysis.

hints.prompt: When a cell cannot use an electron transport chain to reoxidize NADH, fermentation steps in to accomplish what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Recycle the electron carrier back to its oxidized form so glycolysis can keep going

choices.label: B
choices.text: Fully oxidize glucose to CO2 and water

choices.label: C
choices.text: Directly synthesize large amounts of ATP by itself

hints.rationale: Fermentation reoxidizes the carrier (regenerating its oxidized form) to sustain glycolysis.

hints.prompt: Which two options misstate fermentation's purpose - one because fermentation does NOT itself produce ATP, and one because fermentation CONSUMES (not makes) that reduced carrier?

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'to make NAD+' and 'to make carbon molecule intermediates for anabolism'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'to make NADH' and 'to make carbon molecule intermediates for anabolism'

hints.rationale: Fermentation makes no ATP itself and it uses up NADH rather than producing it.

questions.stem: Which molecule typically serves as the final electron acceptor during fermentation?
questions.explanation: In fermentation an organic molecule such as pyruvate (or a derivative of it) serves as the final electron acceptor, regenerating NAD+.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 8 Microbial Metabolism — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-8), Ch.8 MC Q16 = C.

hints.prompt: What is the main role of fermentation for a cell that cannot use oxygen?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To regenerate NAD+ so glycolysis can continue

hints.rationale: Fermentation regenerates NAD+ (the coenzyme) so glycolysis can keep running.

hints.prompt: Can oxygen serve as the final electron acceptor in fermentation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No - fermentation is anaerobic and does not use oxygen

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes - oxygen is always the acceptor

hints.rationale: Fermentation is anaerobic, so oxygen is not its electron acceptor.

hints.prompt: Which two options can be eliminated - one because it is the acceptor for AEROBIC respiration, and one because it is the coenzyme that fermentation REGENERATES rather than uses as the terminal acceptor?
hints.rationale: Oxygen is the aerobic acceptor and NAD+ is the regenerated coenzyme; neither is fermentation's terminal acceptor.

questions.stem: Which fermentation product is important for making bread rise?
questions.explanation: Carbon dioxide (CO2) released by yeast during alcoholic fermentation forms gas bubbles that make bread dough rise.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 8 Microbial Metabolism — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-8), Ch.8 MC Q17 = B.

hints.prompt: What physically makes bread dough rise?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A gas produced inside the dough forming expanding bubbles

choices.label: B
choices.text: A liquid alcohol soaking the dough

choices.label: D
choices.text: Solid crystals forming in the dough

hints.rationale: A gas trapped as bubbles expands the dough, making it rise.

hints.prompt: Yeast carrying out alcoholic fermentation release which kind of product that becomes trapped in dough?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A gas given off as a byproduct of fermentation

hints.rationale: Yeast fermentation releases a gas that leavens the dough.

hints.prompt: Which two products can be eliminated because, at baking conditions, one is a liquid alcohol and the other is a nonvolatile acid (neither forms the leavening bubbles)?
hints.rationale: Ethanol is a liquid and lactic acid is a nonvolatile acid; neither is the gas that leavens bread.

questions.stem: Which of the following is not a commercially important fermentation product?
questions.explanation: Pyruvate is a central metabolic intermediate, not a commercial fermentation end product. Ethanol, butanol, and penicillin are all commercially produced via microbial fermentation.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 8 Microbial Metabolism — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-8), Ch.8 MC Q18 = B.

hints.prompt: Commercial fermentation products are the END products harvested from microbes. How does an END product differ from a metabolic INTERMEDIATE?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An end product accumulates and is collected; an intermediate is quickly consumed within the pathway

choices.label: C
choices.text: Intermediates are always toxic and end products never are

hints.rationale: End products accumulate for harvest, while intermediates are transient within metabolism.

hints.prompt: Which listed substance is a CENTRAL metabolic intermediate that cells rapidly convert further, rather than a harvested end product?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The three-carbon hub molecule at the end of glycolysis

choices.label: D
choices.text: A solvent alcohol used in industry

hints.rationale: The glycolytic hub molecule (pyruvate) is a transient intermediate, not a harvested product.

hints.prompt: Which two products are alcohols/solvents manufactured commercially by fermentation, so neither is the exception?
hints.rationale: Ethanol and butanol are commercially fermented alcohols/solvents.

questions.stem: Which of the following molecules is not produced during the breakdown of phospholipids?
questions.explanation: Phospholipid degradation releases glycerol, fatty acids, and (after further catabolism) acetyl groups. Glucose is not a product of phospholipid breakdown.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 8 Microbial Metabolism — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-8), Ch.8 MC Q19 = A.

hints.prompt: A phospholipid's core is a glycerol backbone bonded to fatty acid tails (plus a phosphate head). Hydrolyzing it directly releases which components?
hints.rationale: Hydrolysis of a phospholipid frees its glycerol backbone and fatty acids.

hints.prompt: After they are freed, fatty acids are further broken down for energy by which process?
hints.rationale: Free fatty acids are catabolized by beta-oxidation.

hints.prompt: Which two are the IMMEDIATE structural pieces released when a phospholipid is hydrolyzed, so both ARE produced (not the exception)?
hints.rationale: Hydrolysis of a phospholipid directly frees glycerol and fatty acids.

questions.stem: Caseinase is which type of enzyme?
questions.explanation: Caseinase is secreted from the cell to break down casein (a milk protein) in the environment, making it an extracellular protease.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 8 Microbial Metabolism — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-8), Ch.8 MC Q20 = C.

hints.prompt: Enzymes are usually named for the substrate they act on plus '-ase.' What does the name 'caseinase' tell you it acts upon?
hints.rationale: 'Caseinase' acts on casein.

hints.prompt: Given that casein is a milk PROTEIN, caseinase belongs to which functional class of enzymes?
hints.rationale: Because casein is a protein, caseinase is a protease.

hints.prompt: Which two options can be eliminated because they act on LIPIDS, not on a protein like casein?
hints.rationale: Phospholipases and lipases act on lipids, whereas casein is a protein.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the first step in triglyceride degradation?
questions.explanation: Triglyceride catabolism begins when lipases hydrolyze and remove the fatty acids from the glycerol backbone; the freed fatty acids are then degraded by β-oxidation.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 8 Microbial Metabolism — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-8), Ch.8 MC Q21 = A.

hints.prompt: What are the components of a triglyceride?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A glycerol backbone and three fatty acid chains

hints.rationale: A triglyceride is glycerol bonded to three fatty acids.

hints.prompt: Two of the answer options describe processes for entirely different molecules. Which process applies to PROTEINS, not triglycerides?
hints.rationale: Forming smaller peptides applies to proteins, not to triglycerides.

hints.prompt: Which two options describe processes for DIFFERENT molecules (ring compounds or proteins), not triglycerides, so both can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: breakage of fused rings and formation of smaller peptides

choices.label: B
choices.text: removal of fatty acids and beta-oxidation

choices.label: C
choices.text: removal of fatty acids and breakage of fused rings

choices.label: D
choices.text: beta-oxidation and formation of smaller peptides

hints.rationale: Fused rings occur in steroids and peptides in proteins; neither relates to triglyceride breakdown.

questions.stem: During the light-dependent reactions, which molecule loses an electron?
questions.explanation: Light energy funneled to the reaction-center pigment excites it enough that it loses (donates) an electron to the electron transport chain, beginning the light-dependent reactions.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 8 Microbial Metabolism — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-8), Ch.8 MC Q22 = B.

hints.prompt: A photosystem contains many antenna (light-harvesting) pigments and a reaction center. Together, all of these are made of what kind of molecule?
hints.rationale: Antenna and reaction-center components are both pigments.

hints.prompt: The stem asks which molecule LOSES an electron. Why can NADPH be ruled out?

choices.label: A
choices.text: NADPH is a product that has GAINED electrons (it is the reduced carrier), so it is not losing one here

choices.label: B
choices.text: NADPH is a pigment in the photosystem

choices.label: D
choices.text: NADPH is part of the cell wall

hints.rationale: NADPH is the reduced product (it has gained electrons), not an electron donor.

hints.prompt: Which two options are eliminated because they are not pigments at all - one being a reduced product and the other a carbon-cycle intermediate?

choices.label: B
choices.text: a light-harvesting pigment molecule and a reaction center pigment molecule

choices.label: C
choices.text: NADPH and a reaction center pigment molecule

hints.rationale: NADPH (a reduced product) and 3-phosphoglycerate (a Calvin-cycle intermediate) are not pigments, so neither loses the initial electron.

questions.stem: In prokaryotes, in which direction are hydrogen ions pumped by the electron transport system of photosynthetic membranes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: to the outside of the plasma membrane

choices.label: B
choices.text: to the inside (cytoplasm) of the cell

choices.label: D
choices.text: to the intermembrane space of the chloroplast

questions.explanation: Photosynthetic prokaryotes lack chloroplasts; their photosynthetic electron transport pumps H+ to the outside of the plasma membrane, establishing the proton motive force used to make ATP.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 8 Microbial Metabolism — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-8), Ch.8 MC Q23 = A.

hints.prompt: Photosynthetic prokaryotes such as cyanobacteria differ from plant cells in that they lack which structure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Chloroplasts (and thus a stroma and a chloroplast intermembrane space)

hints.rationale: Prokaryotes have no chloroplasts, so no stroma or chloroplast intermembrane space.

hints.prompt: In a prokaryote, the photosynthetic electron transport system builds a proton motive force the same way the respiratory one does. Where is this proton motive force established?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Across a chloroplast thylakoid inside a stroma

hints.rationale: Prokaryotes build the proton motive force across their plasma membrane, having no organelle membranes.

hints.prompt: Which two answer options can be eliminated because they name CHLOROPLAST compartments that prokaryotes do not have?

choices.label: A
choices.text: to the stroma and to the intermembrane space of the chloroplast

choices.label: B
choices.text: to the outside of the plasma membrane and to the inside of the cell

choices.label: C
choices.text: to the stroma and to the outside of the plasma membrane

choices.label: D
choices.text: to the intermembrane space of the chloroplast and to the inside of the cell

hints.rationale: The stroma and chloroplast intermembrane space are chloroplast structures absent in prokaryotes.

questions.stem: Which of the following does not occur during cyclic photophosphorylation in cyanobacteria?
questions.explanation: Cyclic photophosphorylation recycles electrons through photosystem I and the ETS to make ATP, but it does not reduce NADP+, so no NADPH is formed.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 8 Microbial Metabolism — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-8), Ch.8 MC Q24 = D.

hints.prompt: What is the defining feature of CYCLIC (as opposed to noncyclic) photophosphorylation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Electrons cycle back to the same photosystem instead of being passed to a final acceptor

choices.label: C
choices.text: It splits water to release oxygen

choices.label: D
choices.text: It occurs only in complete darkness

hints.rationale: In the cyclic path, electrons return to the photosystem rather than going to a terminal acceptor.

hints.prompt: Why do cyanobacteria run cyclic photophosphorylation in addition to the normal (noncyclic) path?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To generate additional ATP when more ATP than reduced carrier is needed

hints.rationale: Cyclic flow provides extra ATP to balance the cell's ATP needs.

hints.prompt: Which two processes clearly DO occur during cyclic photophosphorylation, so neither can be the exception?

choices.label: A
choices.text: electron transport through an ETS and ATP synthesis

choices.label: C
choices.text: electron transport through an ETS and NADPH formation

choices.label: D
choices.text: photosystem I use and NADPH formation

hints.rationale: Cyclic flow moves electrons through an ETS and generates ATP; both occur.

questions.stem: Which of the following are two products of the light-dependent reactions?
questions.explanation: The light-dependent reactions produce ATP and NADPH (and release O2 from water); these energy carriers then drive carbon fixation in the Calvin cycle.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 8 Microbial Metabolism — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-8), Ch.8 MC Q25 = B.

hints.prompt: The light-dependent reactions convert light energy into which kind of chemical products?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Short-term energy carriers used to power later sugar synthesis

hints.rationale: The light reactions make energy carriers (ATP, NADPH) that fuel the Calvin cycle.

hints.prompt: Is glucose a DIRECT product of the light-dependent reactions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No - glucose is built later in the Calvin cycle, not by the light reactions

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes - the light reactions directly output glucose

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes - glucose is split by light

choices.label: D
choices.text: Glucose is never involved in photosynthesis

hints.rationale: Glucose is a Calvin-cycle product, not a direct output of the light reactions.

hints.prompt: Which two options can be eliminated because they list GLUCOSE, a finished sugar the light reactions do not produce?
hints.rationale: Both list glucose, which is synthesized in the Calvin cycle, not the light reactions.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the group of archaea that can use CO2 as their final electron acceptor during anaerobic respiration, producing CH4?
questions.explanation: Methanogens are archaea that reduce CO2 (using it as the terminal electron acceptor in anaerobic respiration) to produce methane (CH4).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 8 Microbial Metabolism — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-8), Ch.8 MC Q26 = C.

hints.prompt: In microbiology, the suffix '-gen' (as in 'methanogen') indicates an organism that does what?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Glows in the presence of a substance

hints.rationale: '-gen' denotes an organism that generates/produces something.

hints.prompt: The organism in the stem REDUCES CO2 and RELEASES methane (CH4). Based on the '-gen' meaning, it is a methane-what?
hints.rationale: It generates methane, so it is a methane producer.

hints.prompt: Which two options are '-troph' organisms named for the compounds they CONSUME (not produce), so neither generates methane as asked?
hints.rationale: Methylotrophs and methanotrophs are defined by consuming one-carbon compounds/methane, not producing methane.

questions.stem: Which of the following processes is not involved in the conversion of organic nitrogen to nitrogen gas?
questions.explanation: Nitrogen fixation converts atmospheric N2 into ammonia (the reverse direction). Ammonification, nitrification, and denitrification act in sequence to convert organic nitrogen back to N2 gas.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 8 Microbial Metabolism — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-8), Ch.8 MC Q27 = A.

hints.prompt: The nitrogen cycle has steps that bring nitrogen INTO organic molecules and steps that release nitrogen back as N2 gas. These two directions are:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Opposite processes, moving nitrogen in and out of the biosphere

hints.rationale: Nitrogen can be pulled into compounds or released as N2 - opposite directions.

hints.prompt: Ammonification converts organic nitrogen to ammonium, and nitrification converts ammonium to nitrate. In the pathway from organic nitrogen toward N2 gas, these steps are:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sequential steps that ARE part of the conversion toward N2

hints.rationale: Ammonification and nitrification are consecutive steps moving nitrogen toward N2.

hints.prompt: Which two processes are consecutive steps that clearly ARE part of converting organic nitrogen toward N2 (organic N to ammonium, then ammonium to nitrate), so neither is the exception?
hints.rationale: Ammonification (organic N to ammonium) and nitrification (ammonium to nitrate) are steps in the pathway toward N2.

questions.stem: Which of the following processes produces hydrogen sulfide?
questions.explanation: Sulfate-reducing microbes carry out anaerobic respiration using sulfate as the terminal electron acceptor, producing hydrogen sulfide (H2S).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 8 Microbial Metabolism — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-8), Ch.8 MC Q28 = C.

hints.prompt: Hydrogen sulfide (H2S) is generated when microbes use which molecule as a terminal electron acceptor and REDUCE it?
hints.rationale: Reducing sulfate as a terminal electron acceptor yields hydrogen sulfide.

hints.prompt: Reducing a molecule such as sulfate as the FINAL electron acceptor of an electron transport chain is characteristic of which broad category of metabolism?
hints.rationale: Terminal electron acceptors are used in respiration's electron transport chain.

hints.prompt: Which two options can be eliminated because they are PHOTOSYNTHETIC (light-driven) processes rather than a form of respiration that reduces sulfate?
hints.rationale: Both are photosynthetic processes, not sulfate-reducing respiration.

questions.stem: The biogeochemical cycle of which of the following elements is based on changes in solubility rather than redox chemistry?
questions.explanation: The phosphorus cycle has no significant gaseous phase and is driven by changes in solubility (weathering and precipitation), unlike the redox-driven carbon, nitrogen, and sulfur cycles.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 8 Microbial Metabolism — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-8), Ch.8 MC Q29 = D.

hints.prompt: Some element cycles have a major GASEOUS phase driven by redox reactions; others lack a gas phase and are driven by dissolving and precipitating minerals. What drives a cycle that is 'based on solubility'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Weathering (dissolving) of rock and precipitation of minerals

choices.label: B
choices.text: Oxidation and reduction changing gaseous forms

hints.rationale: A solubility-based cycle is driven by rock weathering and mineral precipitation, not redox gas changes.

hints.prompt: Which characteristic identifies the element whose cycle is solubility-based rather than redox-based?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It has no significant gaseous form and is mostly found in rocks and dissolved in water

choices.label: B
choices.text: It readily forms atmospheric gases that are oxidized and reduced

hints.rationale: The solubility-cycled element lacks a gas phase and moves via rock and water.

hints.prompt: Which two elements have major ATMOSPHERIC gas reservoirs cycled by redox reactions, so their cycles are NOT solubility-based?
hints.rationale: Carbon (as CO2) and nitrogen (as N2) have large atmospheric gas phases driven by redox chemistry.
